These expenditures also bypassed the regular procurement process and were not covered
by Disbursement Vouchers. As a result, the above transactions did not bear the approval of
the proper officials. The Municipal Accountant, Municipal Treasurer, and Municipal Mayor
were precluded from certifying that an allotment was obligated for the purpose and supporting
documents are complete; that funds are available; and that the transaction is approved for
payment, respectively, thereby undermining the procurement processes intended by R.A.
9184, and also preventing the Municipality from withholding taxes due to the national
government.
Anent thereto, the disbursements amounting to ₱258,900.00 were not taken up in the books
of accounts of the Municipality. Hence, the expense accounts of the Municipality are
understated by the same amount.
It was also observed that payments were made merely by withdrawing cash from the bank
account of the RHU, and paying the suppliers therewith, contrary to COA Circular No. 97-002
which requires that payment be made by check directly to suppliers. Consequently, public
funds were exposed to the risk of loss or misappropriation.
The above-mentioned disbursements were also not covered by a Certificate of Availability of
Funds (CAF) issued by the Municipal Accountant. The CAF serves as a confirmation that the
agency is ready to undergo procurement, and shows that cash allocation is available and that
the obligation incurred can be actually paid.
The CAF being absent, there was no confirmation that the RHU had money to pay for its
purchases. However, the Audit Team noted that a purchase amounting to ₱111,550.00 for
reagents for the Blood Chemistry Analyzer was made by the RHU on February 11, 2020
despite the lack of funds for the purpose, thus incurring a cash deficit of ₱87,055.92 as shown
in the table below. The goods in relation to this purchase were already delivered by the
supplier, but the balance thereof remains unpaid as of this date due to lack of funds.

Based on the above, procurement was undertaken by the RHU without carefully planning its
actual needs and without considering the actual availability of funds. The deficiencies in the
procurement process noted above were in violation of provisions of R.A. No. 9184, resulting
to the inefficient use and wastage of scarce resources of the Municipal Government.
Such failure to abide by the procurement procedures prescribed in R.A. No. 9184 and lack of
oversight by the concerned officials resulted to lax monitoring and weak controls over

them in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) in order to streamline and properly
monitor the procurement process of the Municipality;
d. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to take up in the books all disbursements of the
RHU;
e. Ensure that all disbursements from the fund are covered by existing cash
allocations, as evidenced by a Certificate of Availability of Funds (CAF) issued by
the Municipal Accountant; and,
f. Ensure that all disbursements adhere to prescribed procurement procedures, bear
the approval of the proper officials and are supported with the complete
documentation required in COA Circular No. 2012-001 for control purposes and also
to ensure payment of appropriate taxes due to the national government.

 Inadequate oversight and internal control mechanisms in the RHU
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Direct the concerned officials to design and implement a process workflow
governing the operations of the RHU to streamline the processing of
transactions. Such process workflow should include the detailed step by step
procedure for each activity/medical procedure in the RHU, the personnel
assigned for each identified procedure, and the amount of fees to be paid, if any.
The approved process workflow should be posted on conspicuous locations in
the RHU.
b. Ensure the design and implementation of a system of internal control to safeguard
the funds and properties of the Municipal government, timely reporting of revenues
and expenditures of the RHU, and address the risk of errors and fraud in the
operations of the RHU.
c. Ensure the availability of adequate funding for the RHU. This would entail curtailing
costs and/or increasing revenues. This means purchasing supplies in bulk in
according with the quarterly requirements of the RHU, as well as regularly
reviewing and updating the prices of medical services provided by the RHU to
reflect current costs and competitive conditions in the locality. Collections from
laboratory fees should be used exclusively to cover the cost of providing health
services in accordance with existing budgetary and procurement procedures.
Meanwhile, to cover the remaining cost of rendering services to the charity patients,
the Municipality will have to make allocations of funds from its annual budget.

implementation of projects listed in the 20% Development Plan despite the
availability of funds. Likewise, we recommend that the Municipal Development
Council, headed by the Municipal Mayor, to evaluate and study the feasibility of the
projects to ensure delivery of the intended services to the public. Require the
specification in the plan as to quarter on which projects are to be implemented.
b. Direct the Municipal Engineer to cause the immediate implementation of the
projects that had been allocated funds in order to fully attain the purpose and intent
for establishing the fund at the soonest possible time, thereby, maximizing the real
value of the money on hand.
c. Ensure that only programs and projects geared towards the attainment of socio-
economic development and environmental management outcomes that partake the
nature of investment or capital expenditures, as mandated by law, will be given
allocation under the 20% Development Fund.

activities. Strengthen the rescue and recovery facilities, manpower and equipment
to ensure readiness in case of urgency.

Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council and Local Development
Council the annual LDRRMF Investment Plan (Annex BB) specifying in detail the
list of programs, activities and projects which include under a separate caption
those projects and programs charged to the continuing appropriations as well as
unexpended LDRRMF of previous years, and implement such projects at the

observations.managementAction: For Calendar Year 2018, the Municipality appropriated a total of P13,574,462.80 for its 20% Development Fund. composition of the balances of the above accounts by verifying prior years’ records
to determine their validity and existence. If this is already impossible, the
Municipality, through the Mayor, with the concurrence of the Municipal Council,
should expedite the submission of the request for the write-off/adjustments of the
Receivable accounts to the Commission on Audit, properly supported by a list of
available records pertaining to the accounts such as financial records showing
that the accounts have remained outstanding for years, certification as to the
extent of validation made on the accounts, and the reasons why the records/documents could not be located.

Also, the Accountant should analyze and determine the validity of the long
outstanding obligations and the source agencies of the funds received. Those found
valid should be paid immediately or the unused balances of the funds be returned
to the government agencies concerned while recorded Payables without valid
documentation and which have been outstanding for two years or more and
against which no actual claim has been filed should be, with the concurrence of the
Municipal Council, be reverted to Government Equity, to clear the Payable
accounts. Henceforth, only obligations with proper and complete documentation
should be recorded in the books of accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
 Treasurer’s Office to prepare and maintain road networks property
cards and to submit Report on Local Road Network;
 Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer with the complete description and cost segregation of road
components for road projects;
 Inventory Committee to conduct inventory count of all the Municipality’s
road network systems and accordingly submit a Report on the Physical
Count of the Road Network System as of the end of the year; and
 Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain the prescribed property
ledger cards for Local Road Networks and disclose the information for
the said property in the Notes to Financial Statements in accordance with
the provision cited under COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November
23, 2015.

We strongly reiterated that the Municipal Mayor require:
a. the Municipal Treasurer to find ways on how to facilitate the preparation
and submission of the duly certified list showing the names of all taxpayers
and the tax due from each at the start of the year to the Municipal
Accountant as a basis of recording in the books of accounts the RPT/SET
Receivables and the equivalent Deferred Income accounts. This way, the
actual RPT/SET Receivables and Deferred Income accounts every
beginning of each year will be reflected in the books of accounts and the
collections will be properly taken up on the concerned accounts; and
b. the Municipal Accountant to make a detailed analysis of the Receivable
accounts and their reciprocal accounts to determine the cause/s of the
discrepancies and effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
 require the municipal officials concerned to strictly observe the austerity
measures being implemented by the government in terms of training and
seminars and exercise due diligence on the use of the limited government
funds entrusted to them. Claims for traveling expenses should be in
accordance with Section 6 of Executive Order No. 248 dated May 29,
1995. Further, avoid expenditures that may qualify to irregular,
unnecessary, excessive, extravagant, unconscionable expenditures.
Otherwise, these expenditures will be disallowed in audit.
 secure the permission or prior authority from the Governor for all his
travel outside the province.
 direct the municipal officials to refrain paying miscellaneous expenses
such as plane tickets, accommodations and seminar fees to travel
agencies/event organizers/consulting firms out of the cash advances.
 require all the municipal officials and employees to attend
seminars/training sponsored and organized by government agencies such
as the Commission on Audit, Department of Budget and Management,
Civil Service Commission (CSC) and other accredited Non- Government
Organizations (NGOs) accredited by CSC.
 instruct the Municipal Accountant and the concerned officials and
employees to submit the duly approved Office Orders, Training Designs,
Programs, Certificate of Appearances and all the other required
documents in the conduct of training, attendance to seminars and the
breakdown of expenses for air fare tickets and accommodations of the
participants otherwise, these will be suspended in audit.

Accountant to record all the cash advances granted as Advances from Officers and
Employees and Advances to Special Disbursing Officer and prepare adjustment for
the erroneous entry made during the year and in the previous years for the

Accountable Officers (AO) under his jurisdiction to immediately liquidate their
outstanding cash advances otherwise the Audit Team will be constrained to issue a
demand letter and eventually advise the former to cause or order the withholding of
the payment of any money due the AO.

Henceforth, ensure the proper granting, utilization and liquidation of all cash
advances within the prescribed period in accordance with the above-cited rules and
regulations.

accounts and to prepare and submit to the Audit Team the schedule of the Due to

facilitate financial statements free of any material misrepresentation that could affect
the fairness of presentation of the accounts in the financial statements of the
Municipality.

Municipal Budget Officer follow strictly the guidelines prescribed by DBM-NEDA-

Gender and Development Plan and Budget and ensure that only those
expenditures/PPAs in the approved plan and budget with identified gender issue be
funded and implemented out of GAD fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Planning
and Development Coordinator to strictly comply with the guidelines in the Joint
DILG and Department of Budget and Management (DBM) MC No. 2017-1 dated
February 22, 2017, on the appropriation and optimum utilization of the 20 percent
Development Fund on projects that partake the nature of investment or capital
expenditures.

Ensure that only capital expenditures are charged to the 20% Development
Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the officials concerned to
submit to the Audit Team, for review and evaluation, copies of contracts and

days from execution/issuance in order that Management could be informed
promptly of any defects/deficiencies found therein, so that these could be
immediately corrected and to facilitate the inspection of the projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer to stamp “PAID” the disbursement vouchers and all of its supporting
documents after payment is made to preclude the re-use of the same and prevent
any possible irregularity that may result therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipality follow strictly the above provisions
of law on the submission of the Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation
(AAPSI) within the specified period to give enough time for the Auditor to review,
monitor and evaluate the action taken by the management on the audit
recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer
and other officials concerned to submit to COA Audit Team the list of all on-going
government projects/programs/activities and those that are for implementation
during the year, as well as the information that the appropriate signboards and/or
public notices have already been posted in accordance with COA Circular No. 2013-
004 within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services
Officer and other officer concerned to submit Request for Inspection/Notice of
Deliveries together with copies of the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) to
the COA Office within 24 hours after acceptance and inspection of all the deliveries
by the management.

on the Utilization of the LDRRMF and submit the same to the Audit Team within

soonest possible time for their immediate and appropriate action.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to make immediate reconciliation of the properties owned by the Municipality as recorded in the Municipal books of accounts as against the inventory report of the Treasury Office so as to establish the reliability, accuracy and existence of the Property, Plant Equipment accounts presented in its financial statements as of December 31, 2016.

Likewise, instruct the Municipal Accountant to documents every process made in the identification of all properties recorded in their books of accounts, and if unaccounted or unidentified items persist, then seek the assistance of proper authority to drop from the books of accounts the items that cannot be identified so as to rid the books of unreliable balances that is affecting the fairness of accounts presentation of Property, Plant and Equipment in the financial statements of the Municipality.

Also, instruct the Municipal Treasurer or her representative to cause the immediate conduct of physical count of all properties recorded under the Special Education Fund to ascertain the existence of these properties.  If properties involved are found no longer serviceable or can no longer be found, then proper procedures in the disposal of properties or relief from accountabilities be sought by the concerned accountable officers, all for the accuracy and reliability of PPE accounts presentation.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit to the Audit Team the schedules of the balances of the said accounts, with the corresponding subsidiary ledgers, as it is one of the functions of his Office to prepare a financial statements free of any material misrepresentation that could affect the fairness of presentation of these accounts in the financial statements of the Municipality.

Likewise, require the Municipal Accountant to include in the Notes to Financial Statements all relevant facts of the accounts’ balances that are not mentioned in the face of the financial statements so that its users would be guided in whatever form that such information is useful to them.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to make appropriate adjusting entries by reverting the transfer of public infrastructure accounts into appropriate Infrastructure Assets accounts so that the Municipality is compliant with the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards (PPSAS) and attain fairness of accounts presentation in the financial statements of the Municipality at any given accounting period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, being also the Chairman of the Municipal Development Council to ensure that all projects funded out of the 20 Percent Development Fund be implemented during the year that these projects were proposed for implementation to ensure that the full intention of development would be attained by all its constituents at the soonest possible time.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Local Development Council to ensure that only programs and projects geared towards the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes that partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures, as mandated by the law and its implementing rules and regulations, will be given allocation under the 20 Percent Development Fund to ensure the full realization of the purpose for which the Fund was created.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require all concerned city officials to adhere strictly with the guidelines set under COA Circular No. 2013-01 for the utilization of LDRRMF, and to stop the practice of charging to LDRRMF the expenses that are not related to and not supportive of disaster risk management activities.  Administrative expenses should be charged to the respective appropriation of the Offices concerned considering that the LDRRMF was only for specific purpose not related to the usual operation of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal LDRRM Officer and the Municipal Accountant to regularly prepare a monthly Report on the Utilization of the LDRRMF and submit the same to the COA Auditor within the period prescribed by the above regulation for immediate audit action so that corresponding result may be brought to the attention of concerned officials the soonest possible time for their immediate and appropriate action.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor strictly comply with the provisions of Joint Circular No. 2015-01 of the COA, DBM, DILG, Governance Commission of the GOCC, and DND on the use of Confidential Funds.  He should require the Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer, and other local officials concerned to implement the correct and proper procedures in the disbursement of Confidential Funds so that the regularity and reasonableness of said expenditure would not be compromised.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer to strictly observe the provisions of RA 7160 and National Budget Circular No. 563 in the appropriation and payment of membership fees and annual dues to various leagues, and ensures that payments of such kind to private organization will not be charged against the Municipality’s funds.

3)    Personnel in charge in preparing the APP should be instructed on how to 	properly prepare the APP on then prescribed format.

observations.recommendation: For effective planning and to ensure that the Municipality’s GAD programs, plans and activities shall at all times give priority to those that will address emerging and/or continuing gender issues and concerns especially of marginalized women we recommended that the Municipal Mayor  issue directives for the reconstitution and/or strengthening of the GFPS with the duties and responsibilities of Chairman and members.  Also, require: Elected officials including various Municipal department heads attended seminars on Disaster preparedness together with some barangay officials and NGO representatives.  the Personnel Officer to ensure that all members of the GFPS including elected local officials and department heads undergo capacity building programs on GAD which shall include but not be limited to: gender sensitivity training (GST), gender-responsive planning and budgeting, gender analysis, gender audit and GAD tools such as the Gender Mainstreaming Evaluation Framework (GMEF), Gender Responsive LGU Ka Ba (GeRLKa Ba) Self-Assessment Tool, Harmonized Gender and Development Guidelines (HGDG) and other succeeding GAD tools; the Local Planning and Development Officer to spearhead the maintenance of the GAD data base and establish gender issues and concerns through conduct of gender analysis, generation and review of sex-disaggregated data, and consultations with gender advocacies and women clientele; and the TWG to prepare GAD Plan based on established gender issues and include programs, projects and activities (PPAs) which address gender issues and concerns of their respective sectors and constituents, specifying the targets to be achieved and identifies the performance indicators that will measure their accomplishments including the budget allocated to finance the PPAs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal officials promised to reconstitute the GAD Focal Person System during the current year 2017, although a GAD Focal Person was appointed in the person of the Municipal Social Worker Officer.  Elected officials including various Municipal department heads attended seminars on Disaster preparedness together with some barangay officials and NGO representatives.  GAD Plan was prepared and submitted to the Regional Office thru the Department of Interior and Local Government (DILG).  Maintenance of GAD data base is still in process.
observations.reason:
Time constraint in the reconstitution of GAD FPS.
Time constraint including limited resources.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor to instruct the MENRO to start implementing the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan.  Consequently, stop the incineration in their site and instead practice recycling this kind of waste.
observations.managementAction: During CY 2016, the Municipality has stopped incineration of garbage.  Instead they practice waste segregation at the household level, and waste recycling.  Controlled-dumpsite was also implemented whereby garbage were dumped in a pit and covered with soil and flattened before another set of waste are dumped then covered anew with soil then flattened again.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Inventory Committee finish the conduct of physical count of PPE and come up with the Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) in CY 2016 and every year thereafter and the same shall be reconciled with the books of accounts.
observations.reason: The Accounting and Supply Office had no ledger and property cards respectively.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that: 

2.1.	2.1 The Municipal Treasurer prepare and submit the reports, accounts and the          complete supporting papers  to the Accounting Office within the deadline.  
	
2.2.	2.2 The Municipal Accountant  submit the  financial reports, Official Receipts,   Disbursement vouchers and its supporting  documents to the auditor  on or before the 20th day of each month.
observations.reason: The delay in the transmission of paid vouchers from the Treasure’s Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that management:

3.1.	3.1 Stop the charging and payment of operating expenses and other expenses of the   district office which are not included in the list of priority expenses chargeable against SEF to SEF. 

3.2.	3.2 Make proper representation for the payment of expenditures for other important operational expenses of public schools but not included in the priority items where SEF may be used and the same should be properly charged to the General Fund of    the   Municipal Government subject to the conditions provided under Section 458(xi) of RA 7160 or to the appropriate budget of the Department of Education, Culture and Sports (DECS).

3.3.	3.3 Ensure that  the  Special  Education Fund be expended solely and exclusively for expenditures provided for under Republic Act No. 5447, Republic Act No. 7160 and DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular Nos. 01 s 1998 and 01-A dated April 14, 1998 and March 14, 2000, respectively.

We recommend that management, upon authorization from Sangguniang Bayan, open a separate bank account in the General Fund for the 20% Development Fund. Upon opening, the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant should be directed to transfer the balance of the 20% development fund amounting to P1,516,982.39.
